content.text: Data Quality Validation with Great Expectations

Data pipelines fail silently more often than they crash loudly. A pipeline might run to completion, write data to a database, and report success — while the actual data contains nulls where there shouldn't be any, values outside expected ranges, or columns that quietly changed type after an upstream schema change.
is a Python framework built specifically to catch these problems before they propagate downstream.
Great Expectations is an open-source Python library that lets you define, document, and validate data quality rules — called
— against your data assets. It works as a declarative testing framework for data: instead of writing ad-hoc
checks scattered throughout your pipeline, you define a structured suite of expectations once, then run them against any dataset at any stage of your pipeline.
you declare what your data should look like, and GX tells you whether it does.

GX supports pandas DataFrames, SQL databases (via SQLAlchemy), Spark DataFrames, and cloud storage formats (Parquet, CSV in S3/GCS/Azure Blob). It works with any Python-based pipeline — Airflow, Prefect, Luigi, or bare scripts — and integrates natively with dbt.
The library went through a major redesign with version 1.x (formerly the 0.18.x line), introducing a new fluent API. This guide covers the modern GX 1.x API.
Without data quality validation, pipelines operate on trust — the assumption that upstream systems always produce clean, well-structured data. In practice, this trust breaks constantly:
A vendor changes a CSV format without notice, dropping a column.
An ETL bug introduces NaN into a previously clean numeric column.
A JOIN produces unexpected duplicates, silently inflating row counts.
A date column starts arriving in a different timezone or format.
A categorical column gains a new, unexpected value that breaks downstream logic.

These failures are insidious because they don't raise Python exceptions. The pipeline finishes, the data lands in your warehouse, and the problem is only discovered days later when a business analyst notices a suspicious number in a dashboard.

Great Expectations solves this by making data contracts explicit and enforceable. You define what clean data looks like, and the framework raises an alarm — or stops the pipeline — whenever the data doesn't match.
Validation runs against raw or transformed data as it flows through the pipeline, before bad data reaches downstream consumers.
Expectation suites serve as living documentation of what a dataset is supposed to look like. Any engineer reading the suite immediately understands the schema, value ranges, and business rules.
GX can profile an existing dataset and generate an initial expectation suite automatically, giving you a starting point rather than writing everything from scratch.
GX generates detailed Data Docs — interactive HTML reports showing which expectations passed or failed, with example values of failing rows. These can be shared with stakeholders and data producers.
The same expectation suite runs against dev, staging, and production data, catching regressions as data volumes and sources evolve.
Works with pandas, Spark, PostgreSQL, Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, DuckDB, and more — often without changing the expectations themselves.
Configuring a GX project — data sources, data assets, batches, checkpoints — requires more upfront work than writing a few
statements. For very small or throwaway pipelines, this overhead may not be justified.

The redesigned API introduced many new concepts (Data Context, Data Source, Data Asset, Batch Definition, Batch, Validator, Expectation Suite, Checkpoint) that must be understood together before it clicks.
Running many expectations against a full Spark or SQL table can be slow. This is mitigated by sampling, but sampling introduces its own uncertainty.
Not a replacement for unit tests.
. It doesn't test whether your transformation logic is correct — only whether the output data meets expectations. Pipeline unit tests and GX serve complementary roles.
GX stores validation results, expectation suites, and Data Docs locally or in cloud storage. Over time, this can accumulate.
Understanding GX requires knowing its conceptual hierarchy. Every piece has a specific role.

is the root object — the entry point for everything GX does. It manages configuration, stores expectation suites, tracks validation results, and generates Data Docs. In GX 1.x, you typically create an
context (in-memory, nothing persisted to disk) or a
context (configuration and results stored in a local directory or cloud storage).

import great_expectations as gx

# Ephemeral context — no files written to disk
context = gx.get_context(mode="ephemeral")

# File-based context — creates a gx/ directory in your project
context = gx.get_context(mode="file")
A Data Source connects GX to where your data lives. GX 1.x defines Data Sources by backend type: pandas, Spark, PostgreSQL, Snowflake, BigQuery, etc.
A Data Asset is a logical representation of a specific dataset within a Data Source — a table, a file, or a DataFrame. It defines
data you're working with, not the specific batch of it.
describes how to slice the data asset into individual batches — one full DataFrame, one file per day, one month partition, etc. A
is the actual chunk of data you validate in a single run.
An Expectation Suite is a named, reusable collection of expectations. You add expectations to it, then run the entire suite against a batch.
Expectations are the actual assertions. Each is a structured object with a type and parameters.

from great_expectations.expectations import (
    ExpectColumnValuesToNotBeNull,
    ExpectColumnValuesToBeBetween,
    ExpectTableRowCountToBeBetween,
    ExpectColumnValuesToBeInSet,
)

suite.add_expectation(ExpectColumnValuesToNotBeNull(column="order_id"))
suite.add_expectation(ExpectColumnValuesToBeBetween(column="amount", min_value=0, max_value=1_000_000))
suite.add_expectation(ExpectTableRowCountToBeBetween(min_value=1000, max_value=10_000_000))
suite.add_expectation(ExpectColumnValuesToBeInSet(column="status", value_set=["pending", "completed", "cancelled"]))
pairs a Batch Definition with an Expectation Suite. A
bundles one or more Validation Definitions into a runnable unit that can also trigger
— like saving results, sending Slack alerts, or generating Data Docs.

validation_def = context.validation_definitions.add(
    gx.ValidationDefinition(
        name="orders_validation",
        data=batch_definition,
        suite=suite,
    )
)

checkpoint = context.checkpoints.add(
    gx.Checkpoint(
        name="orders_checkpoint",
        validation_definitions=[validation_def],
    )
)

result = checkpoint.run(batch_parameters={"dataframe": df})
Running production data pipelines where data quality failures have downstream impact (broken reports, model training on corrupt data, incorrect billing).
Onboarding data from external or third-party sources where you don't control the schema or quality.
Building ETL/ELT systems that must satisfy documented data contracts with consumers.
Operating in regulated industries (finance, healthcare) where data quality must be auditable.
Collaborating across teams where data producers and consumers need a shared definition of "correct" data.
You need simple, one-off sanity checks in a notebook —
Your pipeline is extremely simple or short-lived and the setup overhead isn't justified.
How to Implement — Step by Step

pip install "great_expectations[sqlalchemy]"         # generic SQL
pip install "great_expectations[postgresql]"         # PostgreSQL
pip install "great_expectations[snowflake]"          # Snowflake
pip install "great_expectations[bigquery]"           # BigQuery

import great_expectations as gx

# For production pipelines: use file-based context
# This creates a `gx/` directory in your project root
context = gx.get_context(mode="file")
Step 3 — Add a Data Source and Data Asset

import pandas as pd

# Pandas data source
data_source = context.data_sources.add_pandas("pipeline_source")
data_asset = data_source.add_dataframe_asset("orders")
batch_definition = data_asset.add_batch_definition_whole_dataframe("orders_full")

from great_expectations import ExpectationSuite
from great_expectations.expectations import (
    ExpectColumnValuesToNotBeNull,
    ExpectColumnValuesToBeBetween,
    ExpectTableRowCountToBeBetween,
    ExpectColumnValuesToBeInSet,
    ExpectColumnValuesToMatchRegex,
    ExpectColumnToExist,
)

suite = context.suites.add(ExpectationSuite(name="orders_suite"))

# Schema expectations
suite.add_expectation(ExpectColumnToExist(column="order_id"))
suite.add_expectation(ExpectColumnToExist(column="customer_id"))
suite.add_expectation(ExpectColumnToExist(column="amount"))
suite.add_expectation(ExpectColumnToExist(column="status"))

# Null checks
suite.add_expectation(ExpectColumnValuesToNotBeNull(column="order_id"))
suite.add_expectation(ExpectColumnValuesToNotBeNull(column="customer_id"))

# Value range
suite.add_expectation(
    ExpectColumnValuesToBeBetween(column="amount", min_value=0.01, max_value=999999.99)
)

# Categorical membership
suite.add_expectation(
    ExpectColumnValuesToBeInSet(
        column="status",
        value_set=["pending", "processing", "completed", "cancelled", "refunded"]
    )
)

# Row count sanity
suite.add_expectation(
    ExpectTableRowCountToBeBetween(min_value=1, max_value=50_000_000)
)

# Format validation (order ID must be UUID-like)
suite.add_expectation(
    ExpectColumnValuesToMatchRegex(
        column="order_id",
        regex=r"^[0-9a-f]{8}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{12}$"
    )
)
Step 5 — Create a Validation Definition and Checkpoint

from great_expectations import ValidationDefinition, Checkpoint

validation_def = context.validation_definitions.add(
    ValidationDefinition(
        name="orders_validation",
        data=batch_definition,
        suite=suite,
    )
)

checkpoint = context.checkpoints.add(
    Checkpoint(
        name="orders_checkpoint",
        validation_definitions=[validation_def],
    )
)
Step 6 — Run Validation in Your Pipeline
Step 7 — Generate Data Docs (Optional but Recommended)

context.build_data_docs()
context.open_data_docs()  # Opens HTML report in browser
GX ships with over 50 built-in expectations. Here are the most commonly used ones:

ExpectColumnValuesToNotBeNull(column="email")
ExpectColumnValuesToBeNull(column="deleted_at")  # soft-deleted rows
# Allow up to 5% nulls:
ExpectColumnValuesToNotBeNull(column="phone", mostly=0.95)

ExpectColumnMeanToBeBetween(column="price", min_value=10.0, max_value=500.0)
ExpectColumnMedianToBeBetween(column="age", min_value=20, max_value=60)
ExpectColumnStdevToBeBetween(column="revenue", min_value=0, max_value=5000)
parameter (a float from 0 to 1) that sets a minimum pass rate. This is critical for real-world data where 100% perfection is rarely achievable:

# Pass if at least 99% of rows have non-null phone numbers
ExpectColumnValuesToNotBeNull(column="phone", mostly=0.99)
An e-commerce company runs a daily Airflow pipeline that ingests raw order data from an API, transforms it, and loads it into Snowflake for reporting. GX is integrated at two stages:

# gx/suites/raw_orders_suite.py
raw_suite_expectations = [
    ExpectTableRowCountToBeBetween(min_value=500),  # catch empty or near-empty responses
    ExpectColumnToExist(column="id"),
    ExpectColumnToExist(column="created_at"),
    ExpectColumnValuesToNotBeNull(column="id"),
    ExpectColumnValuesToNotBeNull(column="created_at"),
    ExpectColumnValuesToMatchRegex(column="id", regex=r"^\d{8,12}$"),
]

# gx/suites/transformed_orders_suite.py
transformed_suite_expectations = [
    ExpectColumnValuesToNotBeNull(column="customer_id"),
    ExpectColumnValuesToBeBetween(column="total_usd", min_value=0.01, max_value=100_000),
    ExpectColumnValuesToBeInSet(column="currency", value_set=["USD", "EUR", "GBP", "AUD"]),
    ExpectColumnValuesToBeUnique(column="order_id"),
    ExpectColumnPairValuesAToBeGreaterThanB(column_A="updated_at", column_B="created_at"),
    # At least 95% of orders should have a shipping address
    ExpectColumnValuesToNotBeNull(column="shipping_address", mostly=0.95),
]

At scale — processing 2 million rows per day — the team uses SQL-native execution against Snowflake instead of pulling data into pandas. GX pushes the SQL down to Snowflake, so only metadata and statistics are returned to Python. This makes validation fast even on very large datasets.
For database-backed pipelines, GX executes expectations directly in SQL — no data is transferred to Python:

import great_expectations as gx

context = gx.get_context(mode="ephemeral")

# Connect to PostgreSQL
pg_source = context.data_sources.add_postgres(
    name="prod_postgres",
    connection_string="postgresql+psycopg2://user:password@host:5432/orders_db"
)

# Point to a specific table
orders_asset = pg_source.add_table_asset(
    name="orders",
    table_name="public.orders"
)

batch_def = orders_asset.add_batch_definition_whole_table("orders_batch")

# Expectations work identically — GX translates them to SQL under the hood
suite = context.suites.add(gx.ExpectationSuite(name="db_orders_suite"))
suite.add_expectation(ExpectColumnValuesToNotBeNull(column="order_id"))
suite.add_expectation(ExpectColumnValuesToBeBetween(column="amount", min_value=0))

validation_def = context.validation_definitions.add(
    gx.ValidationDefinition(
        name="db_validation",
        data=batch_def,
        suite=suite,
    )
)

checkpoint = context.checkpoints.add(
    gx.Checkpoint(name="db_checkpoint", validation_definitions=[validation_def])
)

result = checkpoint.run()
print(result.success)

An ephemeral context loses all state between runs — no stored suites, no historical results, no Data Docs. Production pipelines should use a file-based or cloud-based context so history accumulates and results can be reviewed.
Putting all validation at the very end of a long pipeline means you don't know which stage introduced the bad data. Validate at ingestion, after each major transformation, and before loading.
can never be null, don't use
— use the strict null check. Reserve

As your data evolves — new columns, new categories, expanded value ranges — expectation suites must be updated to reflect the new reality. Stale suites generate false alarms that get ignored over time, eroding trust in the framework.
reflect the data at the moment of profiling. If you profile on a week with unusually low order volume, the row count expectation will reject normal weeks.

If a pipeline is configured to continue even when validation fails, the failures accumulate unnoticed. Every validation failure should be logged, alerted, and ideally halt the pipeline or route data to a quarantine zone.
Pulling large SQL tables into pandas for validation.
Always use GX's SQL or Spark backends for large datasets. Pulling a 100-million-row table into a pandas DataFrame just to validate it defeats the purpose of having a database.
Start with the most critical columns.
Don't try to cover every column on day one. Start with primary keys, foreign keys, nullability on essential fields, and rough row count bounds. Expand coverage incrementally.
Store suite definitions in JSON or YAML files in your Git repository. This makes suite changes auditable and reviewable via pull requests.
Automate suite updates with data profiling, but review the output.
GX's profiler can suggest new expectations when data changes, but always review suggestions before adding them — the profiler infers from observed data and can encode bad data patterns as "correct."
. Set thresholds based on actual business requirements, not guesswork. Track what the actual pass rate is over time and tighten thresholds as data quality improves.
Separate suites by stage and purpose.
Have one suite for raw ingested data, another for post-transformation data, another for the final load target. Each suite reflects the expectations appropriate to that data's state.
A checkpoint Action can send Slack messages, email alerts, or write results to a monitoring system. Don't rely on engineers manually reviewing Data Docs.
Use Data Docs as a communication tool.
Share the HTML Data Docs URL with data producers (upstream teams, vendors) so they can see exactly which expectations their data is failing. This makes cross-team debugging much faster.
Quarantine failing data rather than discarding it.
When validation fails, route the failing rows to a separate "quarantine" table or file rather than dropping them. This allows investigation and reprocessing once the root cause is fixed.
, GX loads the full DataFrame into memory and evaluates expectations column by column. This is efficient for DataFrames that fit in memory but won't scale to hundreds of millions of rows.

(PostgreSQL, Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, DuckDB), GX compiles expectations into SQL queries and executes them engine-side. Only aggregated statistics are returned to Python. This scales to billions of rows with no data transfer overhead.
, GX wraps expectations as Spark operations and distributes computation across the cluster. This handles petabyte-scale data but requires a Spark environment.
is available for pandas and can significantly speed up validation at the cost of statistical uncertainty:

For large pipelines, a practical pattern is to run the full suite on a sample during development and on every run, but run row-count and schema checks exhaustively (since these are cheap SQL queries regardless of scale).
— Managed SaaS version of Great Expectations with a web UI for browsing Data Docs, managing suites, and viewing historical results across runs without self-hosting.
— GX ships a native dbt integration. Run GX checkpoints after
to validate model outputs. Alternatively, dbt's own
block provides simpler (but less expressive) data tests that can complement GX.
— GX integrates naturally as a PythonOperator or task group. There's also a community-maintained
— Both orchestrators have documented GX integration patterns. Dagster has native asset checks that can call GX checkpoints.
— An alternative to GX focused on statistical profiling and drift detection rather than rule-based expectations. Often used alongside GX: GX for hard rules, whylogs for statistical anomaly detection.
— A lighter-weight alternative for pandas-specific schema and statistical validation, with a cleaner API for simple use cases. Less powerful than GX for complex pipelines but faster to set up.

GX is the most complete solution for production pipelines with multiple data sources, stakeholders, and stages. Pandera is better for simple pandas-only validation. dbt tests are best when your pipeline is already dbt-managed. Custom asserts are fine for throwaway scripts.
Never hardcode database credentials in GX configuration files or code. Use environment variables,
, or a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). GX supports environment variable interpolation in YAML configs:
When using a file-based context, validation results and Data Docs are written to disk. Ensure the
directory (which GX marks as git-ignored by default) is not accidentally committed or exposed, as it may contain row-level sample data from failing validations.
Decide explicitly whether a validation failure should

(log and continue). For most production scenarios, raw ingestion failures should halt; certain post-transform failures might be acceptable with alerting. Configure this consistently across all checkpoints.

Running the same checkpoint twice on the same data should produce the same result. GX validation is stateless from the data perspective — the same expectations against the same data always produce the same outcome. This makes it safe to retry failed pipeline runs without side effects from the validation step itself.

File-based and GX Cloud contexts retain full validation history, providing an audit trail of when data was validated, what passed, what failed, and what the sample values were. This is valuable for compliance and incident post-mortems.
look like, and GX enforces it at runtime.
Data Context → Data Source → Data Asset → Batch Definition → Batch
Expectations are composable, reusable, and backend-agnostic — the same suite works against pandas, PostgreSQL, Snowflake, and Spark.
parameter enables practical validation of real-world data where perfect quality is unattainable — use it deliberately with meaningful thresholds.
For SQL and Spark backends, GX pushes computation to the engine — only aggregates are returned to Python, making it fast even at massive scale.
Data Docs serve double duty as
of data contracts, making cross-team communication much cleaner.
The correct integration point is at
of the pipeline (ingestion, transformation, load), not just the end — this pinpoints where bad data enters the system.
